---
title: "Yang-Mills Lattice Gauge Theory - William's Explorations"

bibliography: references.bib

# format:
#   html:
#     code-fold: true
# jupyter: python3
---

::: {.hidden}
$$
\newcommand\mcy{{\mathcal{Y}}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\def\*#1{\mathbf{#1}}
$$
:::

### April 19, 2026

#### Question about Lemma 4.5.37 in Intro to Random Matrices.

Consider the inputs to Lemma 4.5.37 in @Anderson2010.
Let $P_{\* a}$, $P_{\*b}$ be the laws of $\* a$, $\* b$ on $\mathbb R^N$
and $\mathbb R_+^{N-1}$ respectively.
And let $P_{\* d}$ and $P_{\* u}$ be the laws of 
$\* d$ and $\* u$ on $\Delta_N^c$ and $S_+^{N-1}$ respectively.
Furthermore, let 
$g: \R^N \times \R_+^{N-1} \to \R$ be a test function. 
We view $g$ as a function of a tridiagonal 
matrix, parametrized by $\* a$ and $\* b$.

- Question: Does it follow that
$$
\int_{\R^N \times \R_+^{N-1}} g(\*a, \*b) \, d(P_{\* a} \otimes P_{\* b})
= \int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_{+}^{N-1}} g(\*a(\* d, \* u), \*b(\* d, \*u )) 
\, d(P_{\* d} \otimes P_{\* u})?
$$ 

Next, if the above 
question is answered affirmatively, 
how can I argue that it does hold? 
As far as I know, evidence that is does hold goes as follows. 
First $P_{\* a} \otimes P_{\* b}$ is the law of the entries
of a tridiagonal $\beta = 1$ ensemble matrix; let it be denoted
by $H$. We know that $H = (H_1 \cdots H_N) U D U^T (H_1 \cdots H_N)^T$, 
where $X = UDU^T$ is a GOE(N) matrix with $D = \text{diag}(\* d)$, 
$U$ is the unique 
orthogonal matrix with positive first row,
which happens to be $\* u$, and $H_i$ are the successive
Householder transformations employed in the proof of Thm 4.5.35. 
By the form of the $H_i$, with left most column all zeros except for the top entry, 
and top most row equal to all zeros except for the left most entry,
it follows that the first row of $(H_1 \cdots H_N) U$ is equal to 
the first row of $U$. Hence the first row of $(H_1 \cdots H_N) U$
is also $\* u$. Also, by the bijective correspondence between
$(\* a, \*b)$ and $(\* d, \* u)$, it seems true that 
$\* u$ parametrizes the orthogonal matrices $O$ involved in the 
decomposition $H = ODO^T$.
To say this in another way, let 
\begin{align*}
A &= \{O \in O(N): O \text{ has positive first row and there exists a}\\
& \text{diagonal matrix } D \text{ with diagonal } \*d \in \Delta_N^c \text{ such that } \\
& ODO^T \text{ is a tridiagonal matrix with positive off-diagonal}\}.
\end{align*}
Then it would seem that 
integrating over $A$ with Haar measure on $O(N)$ is 
the same as integrating over $S_+^{N-1}$ with the law of $\* u$. The upshot of 
this discussion is that we are now in a position more similar to that 
of equation (14.10) in @Kemp2022, for instance.
Now let $C$ be a constant of 
proportionality whose value may change from term to term. 
It seems true,
first and foremost based on the fact that $P_{\* d}$ is the law
of the eigenvalues of $H$, but also based on the computations in 
@Kemp2022 Section 14 or even the proof of Theorem 2.5.2 in @Anderson2010, that
$$
\int_{\R^N \times \R_+^{N-1}} g(\*a, \*b) \, d(P_{\* a} \otimes P_{\* b})
= C \int_{\Delta_N^c \times A} g(ODO^T) 
\, d(P_{\* d} \otimes Haar_{O(N)}(O))
$$
and by the parametrization by $\* u$ discussion, we would also have
$$
\int_{\Delta_N^c \times A} g(UDU^T) 
\, d(P_{\* d} \otimes Haar_{O(N)}(U))
= C \int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}} g(\* d, \* u) 
\, d(P_{\* d} \otimes P_{\* u}). 
$$

Why do I care? 
We can show using other arguments that
\begin{align*}
\int_{\R^N \times \R_+^{N-1}} g(\*a, \*b) \, d(P_{\* a} \otimes P_{\* b}) &=
\int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}} g(\* d, \* u) e^{-\sum d_i^2/4} \prod b_i^{i-1} \cdot J d \* d \, d\* u,
\end{align*}
where $d \* d$ denotes Lebesgue measure on $\Delta_N^c$ and 
$d\* u$ denotes uniform surface measure on $S_+^{N-1}$. 
Meanwhile 
$$
\int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_{+}^{N-1}} g(\*a(\* d, \* u), \*b(\* d, \*u )) 
\, d(P_{\* d} \otimes P_{\* u}) = \int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_{+}^{N-1}} 
g(\*a(\* d, \* u) \Delta (\* d) e^{-\sum d_i^2/4} d\*d d \*u
$$
by the fact that $\* d$ is the diagonal of a GOE matrix $X$
in the proof of Lemma 5.4.37, and $\* u$ is a row in an orthogonal matrix
which is distributed according to Haar measure. 
The above being true for arbitrary test functions suggests
that 
$$
e^{-\sum d_i^2/4} \prod b_i^{i-1} \cdot J = \Delta (\* d) e^{-\sum d_i^2/4},
$$
which would imply Lemma 5.4.37.


#### Unorganized Thoughts about Section 4.5 in Intro to Random Matrices by Anderson

::: {.callout-note title="Lemma 4.5.37 in Anderson"}
The map $(\vec{a}, \vec{b}) \mapsto (\vec{d}, \vec{v})$
from $\mathbb R^N \times \mathbb R_+^{(N-1)}$ to 
$\Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}$ is a bijection, whose Jacobian
$J$ is proportional to 
$$
\frac{\Delta(\vec{d})}{\prod_{i=1}^{N-1} b_i^{i-1}}.
$$
::: 

Proof. The first step is to argue that the above map
is a bijection. Before that, we argue that it is well-defined.

The next step is to evaluate the Jacobian.
We will not proceed rigorously because that would 
require geometric notions. Note that 
$(4.5.2)$ and the definition of the map
above suggest that one could find the Jacobian through
implicit differentiation of a set of algebraic equations.
But there's a slicker way. 
The trick is to proceed probabilistically. 
We view $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ as random
entries of a random tridiagonal matrix
$H \in T_N$ in the case $\beta = 1$, 
where $H$ is obtained from a 
random GOE(N) matrix $X$ through successive Householder 
rotations employed in the proof of Theorem 4.5.35.

Next, except on a set of measure $0$,
the eigenvalues of $X$ are distinct (i.e. simple),
and in this case we can write 
$X = UDU^T$ uniquely where $D$ is diagonal
(with entries $\vec{d}$)
with decreasing entries down the diagonal, and 
$U$ is the orthogonal matrix
with the first row $\vec{u}$ having non-negative
entries. Furthermore, except on a set of measure 
0, the entries of $\vec{u}$ are strictly positive.

Let's fix some notions ahead of the computations.
We let $P_H$ denote the law of the entries of $H$ on
$\mathbb R^N \times \mathbb R_+^{N-1}$.
In other words, by independence, $P_H = 
P_{\vec{a}} \otimes P_{\vec{b}}$, where
$P_{\vec{a}}$ is the law of the random diagonal
vector $\vec{a}$ on $\mathbb R^N$, and 
$P_{\vec{b}}$ is the law of the random off-diagonal
vector $\vec{b}$ on $\mathbb R_+^{N-1}$.
Note that $\vec{b}$ is strictly positive 
except on a set of measure 0 because each entry of 
$\vec{b}$ is a $\chi$ random variable.

We let $P_{\vec{d}}$ denote the law of 
$\vec{d}$, that is, $(d_1 > d_2 > \cdots > d_N)$, on
$\Delta_N^c$. 
We let $P_{\vec{u}}$ denote the law of $\vec{u}$
on $S_+^{N-1}$.

Next, let's write down the densities that are known.

Let $g$ be a test function which depends only on the eigenvalues 
of $H$. 

We have the following equality:
$$
\int_{\mathbb R^N \times \mathbb R_+^{N-1}} 
g(H) dP_H(H) = \int_{\Delta_N^c} g(\vec{d}) dP_{\vec{d}}(\vec{d}).
$$

Let $g: \Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1} \to \mathbb R$ be a test function.
The law of the eigenvalues $\vec{d}$ on $\Delta_N^c$ is denoted
by $P_\vec{d}$, while the law of 
$\vec{u}$ on $S_+^{N+1}$ is denoted 
$P_\vec{u}$. 

By Theorem 2.5.2 and Corollary 2.5.4 in @Anderson2010,
$$
d(P_\vec{d} \otimes P_{\vec{u}}) (\vec{d},\vec{u})= \Delta(\vec{d}) 
e^{-\sum \frac{d_i^2}{4}} \left (\prod_{i=1}^N dd_i \right ) \otimes
\sigma(u)
$$
where $\left (\prod_{i=1}^N dd_i \right )$ denotes 
Lebesgue measure on $\Delta_N^c$ and $\sigma$ denotes 
uniform measure on $S_+^{N-1}$.

As a result, 
$$
\int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}} g(d,u) 
\Delta(d) \, e^{-\sum \frac{d_i^2}{4}}
\, \left (\prod_{i=1}^N dd_i \right ) \otimes
\sigma(u) = 
\int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}} g(d,u) \, 
d(P_d \otimes P_u)
$$

Next, since $d$ and $u$ parameterize the tridiagonal
matrices $H$ with diagonal $a$ and positive off-diagonal $b$,
we see that
$$
\int_{\Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}} g(d,u) \, 
d(P_d \otimes P_u) = 
\int_{\mathbb R^N \times \mathbb R^{N-1}_+} g(H)
\, d(P_a \otimes P_b). 
$$

### April 16, 2026

On March 12, 2026, I got a grip on the change
of variables formula. See that post. 

But now I need more of a shorthand because
the reality is that I don't apply change of variables
that often, so I keep forgetting all of the formalism.

Here's what I usually use. Let 
$T: A \subset \mathbb R^n \to T(A) \subset \mathbb R^n$ 
be injective. We start off with the integral
$$
\int_A f(x) \, d\mathcal{L}^n(x).
$$
We make the change of variable $x = T^{-1}(y)$, 
for example, $x = y + \mu$ or $x = Qy$ for some matrix $Q$.
Then we get
$$
\int_A f(x) \, d\mathcal{L}^n(x) = \int_{T(A)} f(T^{-1}(y)) \, dT_*\mathcal{L}^n (y).
$$
For example, we would get
$$
\int_A f(x) \, d\mathcal{L}^n(x) = \int_{Q^{-1}(A)} f(Qy) \, dQ^{-1}_*\mathcal{L}^n (y).
$$
Then the pushforward has a Radon Nikodym derivative: 
$$
\frac{dT_* \mathcal{L}^{n}}{d \mathcal{L}^n}(y) = |\det D_{|_y} T^{-1}|,
$$
so we get 
$$
\int_A f(x) \, d\mathcal{L}^n(x) = 
\int_{T(A)} f(T^{-1}(y)) \, |\det D_{|_y} T^{-1}| \, d{L}^n (y).
$$
In the example, it would be
$$
\int_A f(x) \, d\mathcal{L}^n(x) = \int_{Q^{-1}(A)} f(Qy) \, |\det Q| \, d\mathcal{L}^n (y).
$$

So now let me look into a shorthand notation. 
Consider first using vector notation, and assume 
we are using Lebesgue measure:
$$
\int_A f(\vec{x}) \, d\vec{x}.
$$
Next, make the change of variable $\vec{x} = \vec{x}(\vec{y})$.
Note how, now, $\vec{x} = T^{-1}$.
Then forget about the middle step where
we only had pushforward of $d \vec{x}$
by the map $T: \vec{x} \mapsto \vec{y}$.
Instead jump to the Radon Nikodym derivative step.
Immediately write the Jacobian
$$
J = |\det D_{\vec{y}} \vec{x}|.
$$
Then we get
$$
\int_A f(\vec{x}) \, d \vec{x} = 
\int_{\vec{y}(A)} f( \vec{x} (\vec{y})) \, J \, d \vec{y}.
$$

Let's look at some example. 
Consider the change of variable
$\vec{x} = Q^* \vec{y} + a$.
Then we get
$$
\int_{A} f(\vec{x}) \, d \vec{x} = 
\int_{Q(A - a)} f(Q^* \vec{y}) \, |\det{Q^*}| \, d\vec{y}.
$$

Here's a more complicated example that
I'm working on right now.

We have a bijection
$$
\mathbb R^N \times \mathbb R_+^{(N-1)} \ni (\vec{a}, \vec{b}) 
\mapsto (\vec{d}, \vec{v}) \in \Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1},
$$
where $\Delta_N^c$ is a subset of 
$\mathbb R^N$ and $S_+^{N-1}$ are the points
on the unit sphere in $\mathbb R^N$ whose first coordinate 
is positive. 
Let's denote the forward direction of the above bijection
$$
(\vec{d}(\vec{a}, \vec{b}), \vec{v}(\vec{a}, \vec{b})).
$$
Then in my application we have the 
integral (for some $A \subset \Delta_N^c \times S_+^{N-1}$)
$$
\int_A d \vec{d} \, d \vec{u}  \, C \, \Delta(\vec{d}) \,
\exp \left (- \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{d_i^2}{4} \right).
$$
Basically, we want to replace $d_i$ by 
$\vec{d}(\vec{a}, \vec{b})_i$, and integrate over
$\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$. Let's see what the cookbook
from above gives us.

Step 1. We make the change of variable
$(\vec{d}, \vec{u}) = (\vec{d}(\vec{a}, \vec{b}), \vec{u}(\vec{a}, \vec{b})).$

Step 2. The Jacobian is 
$$
J = |\det D_{(\vec{a}, \vec{b})} (\vec{d}(\cdot, \cdot), \vec{u}(\cdot, \cdot))|.
$$

Step 3. The new region of integration is
$(\vec{a}, \vec{b})(A)$. 

We end up with 
$$
\int_A d \vec{d} \, d \vec{u}  \, C \, \Delta(\vec{d}) \,
\exp \left (- \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{d_i^2}{4} \right) = 
\int_{(\vec{a}, \vec{b})(A)} J d\vec{a} \, d \vec{b} \,
C \, \Delta(\vec{d}(\vec{a}, \vec{b})) 
\exp \left( - \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{\vec{d}(\vec{a}, \vec{b})_i^2}{4}   \right).
$$

The next step is to use Fubini and note that, 
when $\vec{a}$ is a variable
of integration and $\vec{b}$ is fixed, then 
$\vec{d}(\vec{a}, \vec{b})$ varies like...


### April 15, 2026

I need Theorem 6.1 in @Chatterjee2016 but for SU(2). 

Here is one link: <https://www2.math.uconn.edu/~gordina/UniformDoublingSU2.pdf>.
Proposition 3.1 looks relevant. 

In the following link, Section 6, which has a parametrization of SU(2)
and a Jacobian, could also be useful: 
<https://drops.dagstuhl.de/storage/00lipics/lipics-vol152-csl2020/LIPIcs.CSL.2020.34/LIPIcs.CSL.2020.34.pdf>.

The Euler angle parametrization on page 7 of the
following is nice: 
<https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.14397>.

Page 19 of <https://web.math.ku.dk/~jakobsen/replie/compact-work.pdf>
shows how to use a formula for Haar integral with regards to 
SU(2). Page 3 gives a pretty definitive description
of integration over SU(2) with Haar measure 
using spherical coordinates.

Here's Pontryagin's treatment of Top Groups. 
<https://rexresearch1.com/TopologyLibrary/TopologicalGroupsPontrjagin.pdf>.

This link is directly Weyl's formula for SU(2): 
<https://www.math.utah.edu/~milicic/Math_6260/weyl_character.pdf>. 
But I'm not quite sure how to interpret some stuff in there. 

This link discusses computations
for Haar integrals: 
<https://mathoverflow.net/questions/36025/explicit-computations-using-the-haar-measure#:~:text=15%20Answers,unitary%20group%20has%20volume%201.>

Section 4 in these notes are all about SU(2): 
<https://www.math.uzh.ch/gorodnik/lg17/Lie_Groups_Lecture_Notes.pdf>. 

This shows how to recrusively 
obtain parametrizations of SU(2), SU(3), etc: 
<https://arxiv.org/pdf/1708.00735>. 

This link describes Haar measure as well: 
<https://pennylane.ai/qml/demos/tutorial_haar_measure#deguise2018>.

Page 31 of the following has a proof
that su(N) is the Lie Algebra of SU(N), 
and has the Weyl formula's for SO(N), but
unfortunately not for SU(N). But there 
is a general formula that could be interesting.
<https://case.edu/artsci/math/esmeckes/Haar_book.pdf>. 

Computing using Haar measure on SU(2): 
<https://arxiv.org/pdf/1103.3408>. 

See also here for Weyl's formula: 
<http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~wangzuoq/Courses/13F-Lie/Notes/Lec%2027.pdf>. 

Weyl's integration formula: 
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weyl_integration_formula>.
And the Maximal Torus: 
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximal_torus>. 

### April 13, 2026

Here's a Putnam problem that I'm thinking about. 
It is Problem A6 of 1985.

If $p(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_m x^m$ is a polynomial
with real coefficients $a_i$, then set
$$\Gamma(p(x)) = a_0^2 + \cdots + a_m^2.$$
Let $f(x) = 3x^2 + 7x + 2$. Find, with proof, a polynomial
$g(x)$ with real coefficients such that 
i) $g(0) = 1$, and 
ii) $\Gamma(f(x)^n) = \Gamma(g(x)^n)$
for every integer $n$.


Why am I thinking about a Putnam problem?
Well-known mathematicians still publish about them. 
Also Richard Feynman and David Mumford were Putnam fellows.
I've noticed during my 2.5 years in Bonn the difference
between moving too slowly during a math study session
and being dynamic and quick enough. I've brute forced
a lot of study sessions to my own detriment. As I've recently
been more successful in my studies, being dynamic with my 
study sessions has been a key change. But I still 
get stubborn and fall into boring loops and get
pedantic - moving too slow. 
I think Putnam problems will give me a good feel for the mental 
agility needed for research problems. 

### April 12, 2026

Smooth immersions are maps whose differentials
are injective everywhere. Smooth embeddings are 
injective smooth immersions that are also 
homeomorphisms onto their images. Smooth immersions
and embeddings are key ingredients in the theory of
submanifolds. 

The rank of a linear map $L: V \to W$ is the dimension
of its range: $\dim L(V)$. Given two linear maps
$L_1, L_2$, we can find two change of basis 
maps $B_1, B_2$ such that $L_1 = B_1 \circ L_2 \circ B_2$
if and only if the rank of $L_1$ and $L_2$ is the same.

The rank of a smooth map between manifolds
$F: M \to N$ at a point $p$
is the rank of the differential of that smooth
map at the point $p$. 

A linear map is injective if and only if its kernel 
is the $\{0\}$ space. Equivalently, a linear map
$L: V \to W$ is injective if and only if
$\dim L(V) = \dim V$, because then no dimensions
are left for the kernel. 

A smooth map has constant rank if its rank at every point
on the manifold is the same. A smooth map $F$ of 
constant rank also has full rank
if the rank of its differential at all points
is as large as possible: either equal to the dimension
of its domain or codomain. 
By logic, smooth immersions are 
smooth maps of constant rank and 
of full rank whose rank is equal to 
the dimension of the tangent space of the domain manifold.

The mapping $\iota: SU(2) \to \mathbb C^{2 \times 2} \cong
\mathbb R^8$ is smooth provided that for every coordinate system
$\xi$ in $SU(2)$ and $\eta$ in $\mathbb C^{2 \times 2}$, the coordinate
expression $\eta \circ \iota \circ \xi^{-1}$ is Euclidean smooth
and defined on an open set of $\mathbb R^3$.

We have $C^{\infty}(M) = \{f: M \to \mathbb R: f \text{ smooth in the 
sense of manifold theory} \}$. 

Let $M$ be a topological space and let $N \subset M$. 
The subspace topology on $N$ is the collection: 
$\{ N \cap U: U \text{ is open in } M\}$.

Let $E$ be a module over a commutative ring $R$. 
Let $g: E \times E \to R$ be a map. 
If $g$ is bilinear, we call $g$ a symmetric 
form if $g(x,y) = g(y,x)$ for all $x,y \in E$.
If $R$ has an automorphism of order 2
written $a \mapsto \overline{a}$, we say $g$
is a Hermitian form if it is linear in its 
first variable, antilinear in its second, and 
$g(x,y) = \overline{g(y,x)}$. In either case, we call
$g$ a scalar product.

What is a left-invariant vector field on $SU(2)$?
Why does the tangent space 
$T_A SU(2) = A \cdot \mathfrak{su}(2)$
for $A \in SU(2)$?

Let $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ denote the set of all 
smooth vector fields on $M$. Note that 
$C^\infty(M)$ is a commutative ring, 
with addition given by pointwise addition
of functions, and multiplication given by pointwise
multiplication of functions. It is
not a field because a smooth function
which takes the value $0$ somewhere 
is not invertible in the sense 
of multiplication. Then $\mathfrak{X}(M)$
is a $C^\infty(M)$-module. To verify this, think
about why $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is a group
under pointwise addition. Then think about
how a smooth function $f \in C^{\infty}(M)$ acts
on a vector field, and think about why $(f + g)V =
fV + gV$ follows from pointwise considerations.
This module has a basis given by
the smooth sections
$(\partial_i)_{i = 1}^n$. So 
the coefficients in a linear combination would be smooth
functions on $M$.

A derivation on $C^{\infty}(M)$ is a function
$D: C^{\infty}(M) \to C^{\infty}(M)$ that is 
$\mathbb R$-linear and Leibnizian. 
If $V \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ is a vector field, 
then the map $\Psi: C^{\infty}(M) \ni f \mapsto Vf \in C^{\infty}(M)$
is a derivation.
Let's check that $\Psi$ is Leibnizian. 
Let $f,g \in C^{\infty}(M)$. 
Fix $p \in M$. Then $V_p \in T_p M$. By definition 
of tangent vector at $p$, we note that 
$\Psi(fg)(p) = V_p(fg) = f(p) V_p(g) + V_p(f) g(p) 
= f(p) \Psi(g)(p) + \Psi(f)(p) g(p),$
so the Leibnizian property holds pointwise. 

Every derivation comes from a vector field.
Fix a derivation $D: C^{\infty}(M) \to C^{\infty}(M)$.
Notice that when $p$ is a point on the manifold $M$, then
$C^{\infty}(M) \ni f \mapsto (Df)(p) \in \mathbb R$ 
if a rule assigning functions to real numbers. 
Recall that a vector field $V \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, 
at each point on the manifold, assigns functions
to real numbers. So we suppose that
if $V$ does exist, then it should satisfy
$V_p f = (Df)(p)$. The class of smooth functions are
rich enough to determine $V_p$ based on this requirement.

How do we view $Vf$ as a smooth function when $V$ is a
vector field and when $f$ is a smooth function?

How can we consider vector fields as derivations 
$C^{\infty}(M) \to C^{\infty}(M)$?

### April 9, 2026

Consider section 1.4 in @chatterjee2026.
 
- Question: Can you write down a formula
for the leading order term of the free energy using
techniques from @Chatterjee2016?

Work on this question especially when my 
log-gases talk is over. (That is, in May).
Quickly resolving this question would
mean solving it by mid-May, after a couple
of weeks of working on it.

Outline until September 1, 2026. 

1. Go through Section 11 and Section 17 of @Chatterjee2016 
step by step by the end of April.
2. As soon as this is finished, think about how 
to summarize the paper as an introduction section 
to my thesis. Work on this in May.
3. The rest of the thesis
will just be filling in the details of @Chatterjee2016's 
paper in my own voice. Work
on this in May, June, and July.

Thus, the thesis should be finished by the end of July,
more or less, with August to spare.

Also for April: prepare my log-gases talk on May 7th.

July will be busy with the CQFT exam preparation.
April, May, and June will also require some work with
the CQFT course, but less work than the seminar.
